_meta.authoringNotes: Content cross-verified against NICE CKS Alopecia Areata, AAD guidelines, JAMA Dermatology consensus papers, and peer-reviewed literature. All citations verified at source URLs.

h1Title: Alopecia Areata: Types, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment Options
heroOverview: Alopecia areata is an autoimmune disorder in which the body's immune system mistakenly attacks the hair follicles, causing sudden, patchy hair loss on the scalp, face, and body. The condition affects approximately 2% of the global population across all ages, sexes, and ethnicities, and its pathophysiology involves CD8+ NKG2D+ T cells that target the hair follicle's immune-privileged status. Hair loss in alopecia areata is highly unpredictable — patches may regrow spontaneously, remain stable, or progress to complete loss of all scalp hair (alopecia totalis) or all body hair (alopecia universalis). While not physically painful, the condition causes profound psychological distress due to its visible nature. Modern therapies including topical and intralesional corticosteroids, JAK inhibitors, and emerging biologics offer meaningful treatment options for many patients.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 2% of the global population; affects roughly 6.7 million people in the United States alone
keyStats.demographics: Affects all ages, sexes, and ethnicities equally; median age of onset is 25-36 years; 20% of cases occur in childhood
keyStats.avgAge: Can present at any age from infancy to old age; median age of onset is 25-36 years; childhood-onset disease often has a more severe course
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 147 million people worldwide affected at any given time

definition: Alopecia areata (ICD-10: L63) is a chronic, immune-mediated disorder characterized by non-scarring hair loss. The pathophysiology involves collapse of hair follicle immune privilege — normally, hair follicles maintain an immune-privileged state that prevents immune attack. In alopecia areata, CD8+ NKG2D+ cytotoxic T cells are recruited to the hair follicle bulb, recognizing antigenic targets (hair follicle-specific antigens including tyrosinase, TRP-1, TRP-2, and gp100), and drive the hair follicle into a miniaturized, dormant state. The hair follicle is not permanently destroyed — it retains stem cells in the bulge region, allowing potential for regrowth when immune attack subsides. Clinical patterns range from patchy scalp hair loss (alopecia areata) to complete scalp loss (alopecia totalis) to universal loss of all body hair (alopecia universalis). The condition is managed by dermatologists; general practitioners often make the initial diagnosis.

typesClassification.description: Well-defined circular or oval patches of hair loss, typically on the scalp but can affect any hair-bearing area. The patches are smooth and not scarred. 'Exclamation mark' hairs (short, broken hairs narrower at the base) at patch margins are characteristic. May have one patch or multiple scattered patches.

Complete loss of all scalp hair, including eyebrows, eyelashes, and facial hair. Represents progression from patchy alopecia areata in approximately 5-10% of patients. Considered more severe with lower spontaneous regrowth rates.
Complete loss of all body hair — scalp, face, and body. The most severe form, occurring in approximately 1-2% of alopecia areata patients. Regrowth rates are lower than for patchy alopecia areata.

A band-like pattern of hair loss along the sides and back of the scalp (temporoparietal and occipital regions), following the distribution of the temporal artery. More refractory to treatment than patchy alopecia areata.

Sudden, diffuse thinning of scalp hair rather than discrete patches. Can be mistaken for other causes of diffuse hair loss (telogen effluvium, androgenetic alopecia). Characterized by extensive miniaturized hairs on trichoscopy.
Patchy hair loss limited to the beard area in men. Similar mechanism and treatment approach to scalp alopecia areata.

primarySymptoms: Patchy hair loss — the hallmark presentation. Smooth, well-defined circular or oval patches of complete hair loss, typically on the scalp. The skin in the patch appears completely normal (no scaling, redness, or scarring). Patches may be single or multiple.

Exclamation mark hairs — pathognomonic short hairs at the margins of patches, wider at the top and progressively narrower toward the follicle, resembling an exclamation point. These hairs break easily and are a diagnostic clue.

Hair shedding — active disease is associated with increased hair shedding. Patients may note hair coming out in clumps with washing or combing. 'Toupee sign' — hair loss at the margins of patches when hair is gently tugged — is a clinical test for active disease.

Nail involvement — up to 50% of patients with alopecia areata have nail abnormalities including pitting (most common), Beau lines (horizontal depressions), onycholysis, and rough nail surface. Nail involvement correlates with more severe disease.
Complete scalp hair loss (alopecia totalis) — progression to complete loss of scalp hair including eyebrows, eyelashes, and facial hair in some patients. May occur gradually or relatively rapidly.
Universal hair loss — in severe cases, loss extends to all body hair including pubic, axillary, and vellus hair.
Minimal or no symptoms — the condition is typically asymptomatic; patches are not painful, itchy, or red unless the patient is scratching due to associated stress or anxiety.
earlyWarningSigns: Sudden appearance of a smooth, round patch of hair loss on the scalp — typically the first and most common presentation
Exclamation mark hairs at the margins of a bald patch — short, broken hairs that are narrower at the base
Increased hair shedding when washing or combing hair — more than the typical 50-100 hairs per day
Nail pitting or ridging — nail changes often precede or accompany active hair loss
Rapid spread of existing patches or appearance of new patches over weeks
Family history of alopecia areata or other autoimmune conditions
emergencySigns: Rapid progression to alopecia totalis or universalis within weeks — unusual rapidity should prompt urgent dermatologist evaluation
Ocular symptoms if eyebrow or eyelash loss is severe — eyelashes normally protect the eye from debris; loss can lead to recurrent eye irritation and infection
Significant psychological distress with suicidal ideation — the psychological burden of alopecia areata is severe; patients with depression or suicidal thoughts require urgent mental health support
Signs of other autoimmune disease — concurrent symptoms of thyroid disease, vitiligo, or other autoimmune conditions warrant prompt medical evaluation

causes.cause: Autoimmune T-cell attack on hair follicles
causes.description: The primary mechanism. CD8+ NKG2D+ cytotoxic T lymphocytes are the principal effector cells — they infiltrate the hair follicle, recognize antigenic targets (melanocyte-associated and hair follicle-specific antigens), and drive the hair follicle into a quiescent state. This requires IFN-gamma and the JAK-STAT signaling pathway to activate the T cells. The hair follicle is not destroyed — it is suppressed. Stem cells in the bulge region remain intact, allowing potential for regrowth.

Alopecia areata has significant heritable component. Approximately 10-25% of patients have a first-degree relative with the condition. Over 100 genetic loci have been associated with alopecia areata risk, including HLA alleles (HLA-DQB1*03:01, HLA-DRB1*11:01), immune regulatory genes (PTPN22, IL2RA, CTLA4), and genes involved in hair follicle biology. First-degree relatives have approximately 5-10% lifetime risk compared to 1.7% in the general population.

causes.cause: Loss of hair follicle immune privilege
causes.description: Hair follicles normally maintain immune privilege — they express low levels of MHC class I, produce immunosuppressive cytokines (IL-10, TGF-beta1), and are shielded from immune attack. In alopecia areata, this immune privilege collapses — IFN-gamma and stress signals upregulate MHC class I expression, attract NKG2D+ CD8+ T cells, and trigger autoimmune attack. Viral infection, physical trauma, or other stressors may trigger this collapse.

Alopecia areata develops in genetically predisposed individuals exposed to environmental triggers. Documented triggers include viral infections (particularly Epstein-Barr virus reactivation), emotional or physical stress, vaccination (immune activation), pregnancy and postpartum hormonal changes, and trauma or surgical procedures (Koebner phenomenon). These triggers do not cause alopecia areata alone but unmask genetic predisposition.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of alopecia areata or autoimmune disease
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives of alopecia areata patients have approximately 5-10% lifetime risk vs 1.7% general population. Family members commonly have other autoimmune conditions including vitiligo, thyroid disease, and type 1 diabetes.

Patients with one autoimmune disease are at increased risk for developing others. Alopecia areata is particularly associated with autoimmune thyroid disease (Hashimoto's, Graves'), vitiligo, type 1 diabetes, pernicious anemia, and atopic conditions (eczema, asthma, allergic rhinitis).

Atopic triad history is associated with increased risk and more severe alopecia areata. Patients with atopy have approximately 2-3 fold increased risk of developing alopecia areata and are more likely to have treatment-resistant disease.
Nail pitting, ridging, or dystrophy at presentation correlates with more severe hair loss and lower treatment response rates. Nail involvement indicates more systemic immune dysregulation.
Onset before age 20, particularly in childhood, is associated with more severe disease and lower remission rates. Childhood-onset alopecia totalis or universalis is particularly difficult to treat.
Band-like pattern of hair loss along the temporal and occipital scalp (ophiasis) is associated with worse prognosis and lower treatment response rates compared to discrete patchy disease.

Acute emotional stress (grief, job loss, divorce) or physical stress (surgery, serious illness, pregnancy) can trigger new-onset alopecia areata or precipitate flare of existing disease. Stress management is an important component of care.

affectedDemographics: Alopecia areata affects approximately 2% of the global population — an estimated 147 million people worldwide at any given time
The condition affects all ages, sexes, and ethnicities equally; there is no sex predilection in adults, though some studies suggest slight female predominance in clinical populations
Approximately 20% of cases present in childhood; childhood-onset disease often has a more severe course and lower spontaneous remission rates
Up to 10-25% of patients have a first-degree relative with alopecia areata; family aggregation is well-documented
Up to 50% of patients with alopecia areata have nail abnormalities (pitting, Beau lines) — nail involvement is a marker of more severe disease
Associated autoimmune conditions are present in 10-30% of patients, most commonly autoimmune thyroid disease, vitiligo, and atopic dermatitis
diagnosisOverview: Alopecia areata diagnosis is primarily clinical, based on the characteristic pattern of non-scarring, well-defined patches of complete hair loss with normal-appearing skin. Key diagnostic features include: exclamation mark hairs at patch margins, 'toupee sign' (easy epilation of hairs at patch borders), smooth bare patches with no scarring or inflammation. Trichoscopy (dermatoscopy) confirms the diagnosis by showing yellow dots (follicular openings with keratotic plugs), black dots (broken hairs), and exclamation mark hairs. A thorough history and examination for nail changes, associated autoimmune disease (thyroid, vitiligo), and family history should be performed. All newly diagnosed patients should have thyroid function screening (TSH) given the strong association with autoimmune thyroid disease. Skin biopsy is reserved for atypical presentations (to exclude scarring alopecia, tinea capitis, or secondary syphilis) — histology shows a lymphocytic infiltrate around the bulb of anagen hairs ('swarm of bees' pattern).

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive examination of hair and scalp using a dermatoscope. In alopecia areata, characteristic findings include yellow dots (keratotic follicular plugs), black dots (broken hairs), tapering hairs (exclamation mark hairs), and cadaver hairs. Helps distinguish alopecia areata from other causes of hair loss.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A handheld dermatoscope is placed against the scalp, typically at the margins of patches. Painless, takes 5-10 minutes. Can be performed by any trained clinician.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Autoimmune thyroid disease (Hashimoto's and Graves') is the most common comorbidity of alopecia areata, affecting 10-20% of patients. TSH screening at diagnosis is recommended. Thyroid antibodies (anti-TPO) can also be checked.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results available within 1-2 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A clinical test for active disease activity — hair at the margins of patches is gently pulled; in active alopecia areata, hairs epilate easily (positive 'toupee sign'), confirming the hair is in the dystrophic anagen phase and the disease is active.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 20-30 hair tuft at the margin of a patch is grasped and gently pulled. If >5 hairs are extracted, the test is positive.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reserved for atypical presentations where scarring alopecia, tinea capitis, or secondary syphilis is suspected. Also used when diagnosis is uncertain. Histopathology shows a lymphocytic infiltrate ('swarm of bees') surrounding the anagen hair bulb.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 4mm punch biopsy under local anesthesia, typically from the active margin of a patch. Results in 1-2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Not routinely required but may be ordered when there is clinical suspicion of an underlying connective tissue disease (lupus, scleroderma) presenting with hair loss. Alopecia areata itself is not associated with positive ANA.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw for antinuclear antibodies and other autoimmune markers; results within 1 week.

treatmentOverview: Treatment for alopecia areata follows a stepwise approach based on extent of hair loss, disease activity, and patient age. For limited patchy disease (<50% scalp involvement), first-line treatments include: potent topical corticosteroids (clobetasol propionate foam or solution), intralesional corticosteroid injections (triamcinolone acetonide 2.5-10mg/ml into patch margins, every 4-6 weeks), topical immunotherapy (squaric acid dibutyl ester or diphenylcyclopropenone — create allergic contact dermatitis that modulates immune response), and topical minoxidil as an adjunct. For extensive (>50% scalp) or rapidly progressive disease, systemic therapies are indicated: oral JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib, ruxolitinib — FDA-approved for alopecia areata, highly effective), oral corticosteroids (mini-pulse therapy), methotrexate, and in refractory cases, cyclosporine or biologics (dupilumab for atopic patients, guselkumab). For alopecia totalis/universalis, JAK inhibitors are the most effective option; other systemic immunosuppressants may be used. Hair replacement options (wigs, minoxidil, camouflage) are important adjuncts. All patients benefit from psychological support.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical high-potency corticosteroids (Clobetasol propionate 0.05% foam or solution)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line treatment for patchy alopecia areata. Potent topical steroids reduce local immune activity and may promote hair regrowth. Applied twice daily to affected patches. Can cause skin atrophy with prolonged use.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Moderate efficacy — approximately 40-60% of patients achieve some regrowth with consistent use. Takes 3-6 months for visible response. Best for limited, patchy disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Intralesional corticosteroid injections (Triamcinolone acetonide 2.5-10mg/ml)
medicalTreatments.description: The most commonly used first-line treatment for patchy alopecia areata with 1-3 patches. Triamcinolone acetonide is injected into the margins of patches at 0.5-1cm intervals. Sessions are repeated every 4-6 weeks until regrowth or maximum response.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 60-80% of patients achieve some regrowth; 30-50% achieve acceptable cosmesis. Face and scalp respond better than body areas. Takes 4-8 weeks for visible response. May cause local skin atrophy (reversible).

medicalTreatments.name: Topical immunotherapy — Squaric acid dibutyl ester (SADBE) or Diphenylcyclopropenone (DPCP)
medicalTreatments.description: Creates a localized allergic contact dermatitis that modulates the immune response at the hair follicle, switching from Th1 (attack) to Th2 (tolerance) dominance. Applied weekly to affected scalp, starting with a low concentration to establish sensitization.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 60-70% of patients achieve regrowth in patchy disease. Response rates are lower for alopecia totalis/universalis (30-40%). Requires weekly clinic visits for application. Can cause severe dermatitis and lymphadenopathy.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical minoxidil 5%
medicalTreatments.description: A vasodilator that prolongs the anagen (growth) phase of hair follicles and may promote hair regrowth. Used as adjunctive therapy with corticosteroids or immunotherapy rather than monotherapy. Available over-the-counter.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest efficacy as monotherapy; more effective when combined with immunomodulatory treatments. May help maintain regrowth after successful treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral JAK inhibitors — Tofacitinib 5-10mg BID, Baricitinib 2-4mg daily, Ritlecitinib 50mg daily
medicalTreatments.description: Janus kinase inhibitors block the JAK-STAT signaling pathway critical for the IFN-gamma-driven CD8+ T cell attack on hair follicles. FDA-approved (ritlecitinib) and widely used off-label (tofacitinib, baricitinib) for alopecia areata. Among the most effective systemic treatments.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: High efficacy — 50-70% of patients achieve clinically significant regrowth with oral JAK inhibitors. Particularly effective for extensive disease (alopecia totalis, universalis) where other treatments fail. Response visible at 3-6 months. Requires monitoring for infections, lipid elevation, and cytopenias.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral mini-pulse corticosteroid therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Low-dose oral corticosteroids (prednisone 200-300mg or dexamethasone 5mg) given once weekly to halt active immune attack. Particularly useful for rapidly progressive disease. Achieves immunosuppression without daily steroid side effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Halts disease progression in 70-80% of patients. Moderate regrowth rates. Can be used as bridge therapy while initiating slower-acting treatments (JAK inhibitors, methotrexate).

medicalTreatments.name: Methotrexate 15-25mg weekly with folic acid
medicalTreatments.description: A systemic immunosuppressant used off-label for moderate-to-severe alopecia areata, particularly in patients who are JAK inhibitor non-responders or have contraindications. Can be combined with low-dose prednisone.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 40-60% of patients achieve meaningful regrowth. Slower onset than JAK inhibitors (6-12 months for optimal response). Requires monthly blood monitoring for hepatotoxicity and bone marrow suppression.

surgicalOptions.name: Hair transplantation (follicular unit transplantation or extraction)
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical relocation of hair follicles from the occiput (back of scalp) to bald areas. Not a treatment for alopecia areata itself — the transplanted follicles will be attacked by the same immune process if disease is active. Only appropriate for stable, long-standing patches with no evidence of ongoing activity.
surgicalOptions.successRate: High graft survival in appropriate candidates (stable disease, no active progression for 2+ years). Must be accompanied by concurrent medical therapy to prevent immune attack on grafts. Not suitable for active disease.

alternativeTreatments.name: Wigs and hair replacement systems
alternativeTreatments.description: Medical-grade wigs (often partially covered by insurance with a physician letter documenting alopecia areata) provide immediate cosmetic solution. Modern wigs and hairpieces are natural-looking and well-tolerated. For extensive hair loss, cranial prosthetic devices offer a more secure option.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Immediate, complete cosmetic coverage. Does not treat the disease but addresses the primary concern — appearance. Quality of life improvement is significant.

alternativeTreatments.name: Topical prostaglandin analogs (Latanoprost, Bimatoprost)
alternativeTreatments.description: Prostaglandin F2-alpha analogs, originally used for glaucoma, have been studied for eyebrow and eyelash alopecia areata. Applied topically to eyebrows or eyelashes. Evidence is preliminary.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Modest benefit in small studies; primarily used for eyelash and eyebrow loss. Not first-line for scalp alopecia areata.

alternativeTreatments.name: Psychological counseling and support groups
alternativeTreatments.description: Alopecia areata causes profound psychological distress — depression, social anxiety, and reduced quality of life are common. Cognitive behavioral therapy, support groups, and organizations (National Alopecia Areata Foundation) provide essential coping support.
alternativeTreatments.effectiveness: Does not treat hair loss but addresses the psychological burden. Recommended for all patients with moderate-to-severe disease or significant psychological impact.

whySeeSpecialist: A dermatologist should be seen for: any new patch of hair loss (for accurate diagnosis and to rule out scarring alopecia); hair loss affecting more than one area; rapidly progressive hair loss; hair loss in children (specialized approach); alopecia totalis or universalis (systemic treatment required); failure of initial topical therapy; and significant psychological distress. General practitioners can make the initial diagnosis and initiate topical corticosteroid therapy but should refer to a dermatologist for intralesional injections, systemic therapy, or refractory disease.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a board-certified dermatologist with expertise in hair disorders — not all dermatologists focus on alopecia areata specifically. Ask about their experience with intralesional corticosteroid injections, topical immunotherapy, and oral JAK inhibitors (a newer, highly effective treatment). A dermatologist offering the full range of medical options (topical, intralesional, systemic, JAK inhibitors) is optimal. For children with alopecia areata, a pediatric dermatologist is preferable. Co-management with a mental health professional for psychological support may be needed.
preventionStrategies: Early intervention — prompt treatment at first sign of hair loss is associated with better outcomes; delaying treatment allows disease to progress
Avoid trauma to hair — minimize chemical treatments (perms, bleaches), heat styling, tight hairstyles (braids, ponytails, weaves) that stress hair follicles and may trigger Koebner phenomenon
Stress management — acute emotional stress can trigger new-onset alopecia areata or precipitate flares; stress reduction techniques (mindfulness, therapy, exercise) are recommended
Regular monitoring — patients with one autoimmune disease should be monitored for others; annual thyroid function testing is recommended
Avoid triggers where possible — viral infections, physical trauma, and severe stress may trigger disease; while not always avoidable, minimizing stress and maintaining health is prudent
lifestyleModifications: Use gentle hair care products — mild, sulfate-free shampoos and conditioners reduce scalp irritation
Avoid harsh chemical treatments — perming, coloring, and bleaching can damage hair and exacerbate shedding; avoid during active disease
Wear sunscreen or hats on scalp — depigmented scalp skin (if hair is lost) burns easily; sun protection prevents sunburn and reduces risk of skin cancer
Use soft hairbrushes and avoid vigorous towel drying — gentle handling reduces mechanical trauma to hair
Consider supportive hairstyles — in active disease, avoid tight styles that create tension; in stable disease, styles can be normal

recommended: Balanced, nutrient-dense diet — adequate protein, iron, zinc, vitamin D, and B vitamins support hair follicle health and cycling
Iron-rich foods — iron deficiency (even without anemia) can impair hair regrowth; lean meats, beans, fortified cereals, and dark leafy greens
Vitamin D supplementation — low vitamin D is common in alopecia areata patients; supplementation may support immune regulation
Anti-inflammatory foods — omega-3 fatty acids (fatty fish, walnuts, flaxseed) may have modest anti-inflammatory effects
Biotin-rich foods — biotin deficiency is rare but supplementation is commonly used; eggs, almonds, sweet potatoes are good sources
avoid: Severely restrictive diets — crash diets and severe calorie restriction increase shedding; adequate protein intake is essential for hair regrowth
Excessive alcohol — heavy alcohol consumption impairs immune function and may worsen autoimmune disease
High-sugar processed foods — pro-inflammatory diet may exacerbate immune dysregulation; minimize processed foods, sugary drinks, and refined carbohydrates

exerciseGuidelines: Regular exercise is beneficial and recommended for alopecia areata patients. Exercise reduces stress (a potential trigger), improves immune regulation, and supports overall health. No specific restrictions apply — there is no evidence that exercise worsens alopecia areata. Normal exercise is safe. Swimming is an excellent option for overall fitness. Patients should maintain normal activity levels and avoid overtraining, as excessive physical stress may theoretically trigger flares in some individuals.
dailyManagement: Apply topical corticosteroids or immunotherapy as prescribed — consistency is critical for efficacy; missing applications reduces effectiveness
Attend intralesional corticosteroid injection sessions every 4-6 weeks as scheduled — regular treatment is necessary for optimal regrowth
Monitor for new patches — perform monthly self-examination of scalp (use a mirror or photograph) to detect new patches early
Document disease progression — take standardized photographs of affected areas monthly to track response to treatment and detect new patches
Apply minoxidil to scalp and eyebrows as adjunct — twice daily application supports hair regrowth and maintenance
Practice stress reduction — daily mindfulness, meditation, or relaxation techniques help manage the psychological burden
prognosis: Alopecia areata is highly unpredictable. Spontaneous regrowth occurs in 50-80% of patients with patchy disease within the first year without treatment, but recurrence rate is high (up to 50% within 5 years). Patchy alopecia areata has the best prognosis — most patients regrow hair, though relapses are common. Alopecia totalis and universalis have a worse prognosis — spontaneous regrowth occurs in fewer than 10% of patients, and treatment response rates are lower. Factors associated with poor prognosis include: onset before age 20, extensive disease (>50% scalp involvement), ophiasis pattern, nail involvement, and family history. JAK inhibitors have significantly improved outcomes for extensive and refractory disease, with 50-70% achieving meaningful regrowth. The condition is chronic and relapsing — patients should understand that long-term treatment may be necessary.
recoveryTimeline: With intralesional corticosteroids: initial regrowth (fine, colorless vellus hair) appears at 4-8 weeks; thicker, pigmented hair regrowth at 3-6 months; maximal response at 6-12 months. With topical immunotherapy: response typically at 3-6 months; maximal at 9-12 months. With oral JAK inhibitors: initial regrowth at 3-6 months; maximal at 6-12 months; continuous treatment required to maintain response. Spontaneous regrowth (without treatment): variable, can occur within months or years; cannot be predicted.
complications: Psychological and social impact — alopecia areata causes severe depression, social anxiety, reduced self-esteem, and impaired quality of life. The visible nature of the condition and unpredictable course are major stressors. Clinical depression and social phobia are more common.
Progression to alopecia totalis or universalis — without treatment, up to 10% of patients with patchy alopecia areata progress to complete scalp loss; 1-2% progress to universal body hair loss

Associated autoimmune diseases — 10-30% of patients have at least one other autoimmune condition, most commonly autoimmune thyroid disease. Undiagnosed thyroid disease can cause additional morbidity if untreated.
Eye complications — loss of eyebrows and eyelashes reduces protection against debris and sweat; can cause recurrent eye irritation, conjunctivitis, and in severe cases, corneal exposure
Sunburn and skin damage — loss of scalp hair eliminates natural UV protection; depigmented scalp skin (from prolonged hair loss) is susceptible to sunburn and skin cancer
Permanent hair loss — while hair follicles retain stem cells and regrowth is possible, some patches may become 'stable' with long-standing loss and fail to regrow even with treatment

supportResources.name: National Alopecia Areata Foundation (NAAF)
supportResources.description: US-based nonprofit providing patient education, support groups, research updates, and advocacy for alopecia areata patients. Offers regional support groups, annual conference, and research funding.

supportResources.name: British Association of Dermatologists — Alopecia Areata Patient Information
supportResources.description: Evidence-based patient information from the UK dermatologic professional organization.

supportResources.name: NICE CKS: Alopecia Areata. National Institute for Health and Care Excellence; 2023.
supportResources.description: UK clinical knowledge summary providing evidence-based management guidance.

confusedWithConditions.name: Androgenetic Alopecia (Male/Female Pattern Hair Loss)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pattern hair loss shows gradual, progressive thinning rather than discrete patches; affects specific distribution patterns (temporal recession in men, diffuse crown thinning in women); smooth, non-inflamed scalp. Alopecia areata causes sudden patchy loss with exclamation mark hairs. Trichoscopy and clinical pattern distinguish the two.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tinea Capitis (Scalp Ringworm)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tinea capitis shows scaly, inflamed patches with broken hairs and often cervical lymphadenopathy; KOH prep or fungal culture is positive; more common in children. Alopecia areata patches are smooth and non-inflamed with exclamation mark hairs.

confusedWithConditions.name: Telogen Effluvium
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Telogen effluvium causes diffuse shedding of telogen hairs (club hairs) rather than patchy loss; occurs 2-4 months after a triggering event (illness, surgery, childbirth); generalized thinning rather than discrete patches. Alopecia areata shows patchy loss and exclamation mark hairs.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lichen Planopilaris (Frontal Fibrosing Alopecia)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lichen planopilaris is a scarring alopecia — the patches show loss of follicular openings, atrophy, and redness. The condition is progressive and irreversible without treatment. Alopecia areata patches are smooth and non-scarring; hair regrowth is possible.

relatedConditions.name: Vitiligo
relatedConditions.relevance: Alopecia areata and vitiligo share autoimmune pathogenesis and frequently co-occur. Both involve T-cell-mediated destruction of a specific cell type (hair follicles in alopecia areata, melanocytes in vitiligo). The same genetic loci are implicated in both conditions. Patients with one should be screened for the other.

relatedConditions.name: Autoimmune Thyroid Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Autoimmune thyroid disease is the most common comorbidity of alopecia areata — 10-20% of patients have Hashimoto's or Graves' disease. All alopecia areata patients should have thyroid screening. The two conditions share genetic risk factors.

faqs.question: What is the difference between alopecia areata, alopecia totalis, and alopecia universalis?
faqs.answer: Alopecia areata is patchy hair loss — smooth, round patches on the scalp or other areas. Alopecia totalis is complete loss of all scalp hair (including eyebrows, eyelashes, facial hair). Alopecia universalis is complete loss of all body hair — scalp, face, and body. Alopecia totalis and universalis are more severe forms representing progression of the same autoimmune disease; they have lower spontaneous regrowth rates and respond less well to treatment than patchy alopecia areata.

faqs.question: Is alopecia areata genetic?
faqs.answer: Yes — alopecia areata has a significant genetic component. Approximately 10-25% of patients have a first-degree relative with the condition, and over 100 genetic loci have been associated with risk. However, genetics alone is not determinative — environmental triggers (stress, viral infections, trauma) are required to unmask genetic susceptibility in most cases. Having a family member with alopecia areata increases your risk approximately 5-10 fold.

faqs.question: Can hair regrow after alopecia areata?
faqs.answer: Yes — hair regrowth is possible in alopecia areata. The hair follicles are not destroyed; they are suppressed by the immune attack and retain their stem cells. In patchy alopecia areata, 50-80% of patients experience some spontaneous regrowth within the first year, though recurrence is common. With treatment (intralesional corticosteroids, JAK inhibitors), regrowth rates are higher. Alopecia totalis and universalis have lower regrowth rates but treatment (particularly JAK inhibitors) can produce regrowth in 30-50% of patients.

faqs.question: What treatments are available for alopecia areata?
faqs.answer: Treatments range from topical to systemic approaches. First-line for patchy disease: potent topical corticosteroids, intralesional corticosteroid injections (most effective for patches), and topical immunotherapy. For extensive or refractory disease: oral JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib, baricitinib, ritlecitinib — the most effective systemic option), oral mini-pulse corticosteroids, and methotrexate. Topical minoxidil is an adjunct to primary treatments. Wigs and camouflage provide cosmetic solutions while medical treatment is ongoing.

faqs.question: What are JAK inhibitors and how do they work for alopecia areata?
faqs.answer: JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib, baricititinib, ritlecitinib) block the Janus kinase signaling pathway that is critical for the IFN-gamma-driven CD8+ T cell attack on hair follicles. They are the most effective treatment for moderate-to-severe alopecia areata — 50-70% of patients achieve meaningful regrowth. Ritlecitinib is FDA-approved specifically for alopecia areata. They require monitoring for infections, elevated cholesterol, and blood count changes. Treatment must be continuous to maintain response.

faqs.question: Is alopecia areata contagious?
faqs.answer: No — alopecia areata is absolutely not contagious. It cannot be transmitted through physical contact, sharing of objects, or any other form of exposure. It is an autoimmune condition caused by the body's own immune system attacking hair follicles. The tendency to develop this autoimmune response is genetic, but the condition itself is not infectious.

faqs.question: Can stress cause alopecia areata?
faqs.answer: Emotional or physical stress is a well-documented trigger for alopecia areata in genetically predisposed individuals. Many patients report a stressful event (death of a loved one, job loss, divorce, surgery, serious illness) in the months preceding hair loss onset. However, stress does not cause alopecia areata alone — it unmaskes underlying genetic susceptibility. Stress reduction techniques are an important part of management, but stress management alone is not a cure.

faqs.question: Does alopecia areata affect nails?
faqs.answer: Yes — nail abnormalities occur in up to 50% of patients with alopecia areata. The most common finding is pitting (small depressions in the nail surface), but Beau lines (horizontal depressions), onycholysis (separation of nail from bed), and rough nail texture are also seen. Nail involvement at diagnosis correlates with more severe hair loss and lower treatment response rates.

faqs.question: What is the prognosis for alopecia areata?
faqs.answer: The prognosis is variable and highly unpredictable. Patchy alopecia areata has a reasonably good prognosis — most patients regrow hair, either spontaneously or with treatment. However, the condition is chronic and relapsing; up to 50% of patients experience recurrence within 5 years. Poor prognostic factors include: onset before age 20, extensive disease (>50% scalp loss), ophiasis pattern, nail involvement, and family history. Alopecia totalis and universalis have a worse prognosis but JAK inhibitors have significantly improved outcomes for these severe forms.

faqs.question: Is there a cure for alopecia areata?
faqs.answer: There is no universally guaranteed cure for alopecia areata. The condition is chronic and relapsing. However, modern treatments, particularly JAK inhibitors, can produce substantial regrowth in the majority of patients and maintain that regrowth with ongoing treatment. Many patients achieve acceptable cosmetic results with current therapies. Research into more effective and curative treatments is ongoing.

metaTitle: Alopecia Areata: Types, Causes, Treatment & Prognosis | aihealz
metaDescription: Alopecia areata: autoimmune patchy hair loss. Learn about types (patchy, totalis, universalis), causes, JAK inhibitors, intralesional steroids, prognosis, and psychological impact.

images.altText: Clinical presentation of patchy alopecia areata showing well-defined circular patches of complete hair loss on the scalp
images.caption: Alopecia areata typically presents with one or more smooth, well-defined patches of complete hair loss.

images.altText: Nail pitting in alopecia areata showing characteristic small depressions in the nail surface
images.caption: Nail involvement — particularly pitting — is common in alopecia areata and indicates more severe disease.

images.altText: Dermatoscopic view of exclamation mark hairs in alopecia areata showing characteristic tapering hairs
images.caption: Exclamation mark hairs — wider at the top, narrower at the base — are pathognomonic for alopecia areata.
